skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation guidance for AI agents using the agent-browser CLI, snapshot references, screenshots, and repeatable verification workflows.
skill.summary: Automate browser workflows with deterministic snapshot references and verification steps.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell-command and URL patterns, but most are markdown command examples for the documented agent-browser CLI rather than hidden executable code. The skill still has medium risk because its intended use includes network navigation, form interaction, saved authentication state, and optional in-page JavaScript evaluation. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious persistence.

medium_findings.title: External Browser Automation Commands
medium_findings.description: The shell-command findings are largely markdown examples, but they describe real agent-browser commands that open sites, click controls, fill fields, and capture evidence. This is legitimate for the skill, but it can affect external websites if used without domain and action controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly instruct command execution for browser interaction. The intent appears legitimate, so this is a capability risk rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Saved Authentication State Handling
medium_findings.description: The authentication guide fills credentials from environment variables and saves browser state to auth.json. Saved browser state can contain cookies or session tokens, so users need secure storage, environment scoping, and rotation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential and state commands are explicit, and auth state commonly carries sensitive tokens. The same file includes safety notes, reducing malicious concern.

medium_findings.title: In-Page JavaScript Evaluation Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends eval through standard input for complex JavaScript. This can be useful for inspection, but it can also read page content or trigger page behavior in the active browser context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The eval capability is directly documented. The policy example can deny eval, which shows awareness but does not remove the underlying risk.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to example domains and project links in documentation. No evidence found that these URLs receive secrets or are contacted automatically by the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation examples or source links. I found no automatic request logic or exfiltration behavior at these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts point to descriptive skill metadata and a text output example, not cryptographic operations. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, or other weak cryptographic algorithm use in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not perform hashing or encryption. They contain plain documentation text, so the scanner match is very likely spurious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Driven Browser Actions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill centers on running agent-browser commands from a shell to navigate, inspect, and interact with websites.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Command examples are explicit and central to the skill. They are expected behavior, but still represent a meaningful execution surface.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser State Persistence
dangerous_patterns.description: The authentication workflow saves and reloads browser state, which may include session cookies or tokens.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The state save and load commands are explicit. The sensitivity depends on the target application, but the data can be security relevant.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Context Eval
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents eval through standard input for JavaScript executed in the browser context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The eval command is plainly documented. It is not malicious by itself, but it can access page state when permitted.

content.user_title: Automate Browser Workflows with Snapshot Refs
content.value_statement: Manual web testing and browser automation can be slow, fragile, and hard to verify. This skill guides AI agents through repeatable browser actions using snapshot references, waits, screenshots, and diffs.
actual_capabilities: Open web pages and wait for stable page states before interaction.
Use snapshot references such as @e1 and @e2 for deterministic clicks and form fills.
Capture screenshots, PDFs, text, URLs, and structured page snapshots.
Compare snapshots or screenshots to verify UI changes after actions.
Reuse browser sessions and saved authentication state for repeated workflows.
Run isolated browser tasks with named sessions for parallel work.
limitations: Requires the agent-browser CLI to be installed and available in the shell.
Snapshot references become stale after navigation or major DOM updates.
Authenticated workflows can create sensitive browser state files.
JavaScript eval should be restricted on untrusted or sensitive sites.

use_cases.title: Verify a Web App Change
use_cases.description: Open a local or staging site, perform a UI action, and compare the resulting snapshot or screenshot.

use_cases.title: Automate Form Checks
use_cases.description: Navigate to a form, collect element references, fill fields, submit, and verify the expected success state.

use_cases.title: Collect Page Evidence
use_cases.description: Capture screenshots, PDFs, page text, or URL state for audits, bug reports, and repeatable reviews.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the target page with agent-browser, wait until it is stable, take an interactive snapshot, and summarize the visible elements.

prompt_templates.title: Fill and Verify a Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use snapshot references to fill the form fields, submit the form, wait for the result, and verify the confirmation state.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Regression Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Capture a baseline screenshot, perform the requested browser action, compare the result, and report visible changes with evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Restricted Authenticated Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use allowed domains and an action policy, load saved auth state, complete the workflow, and avoid eval, downloads, and uploads.

output_examples.input: Inspect the checkout page and verify that the primary call to action is visible.
output: Page loaded successfully after the network became idle.
The checkout form and primary payment button were visible in the snapshot.
No blocking validation messages appeared before submission.

output_examples.input: Submit the login form using saved credentials and confirm the dashboard loads.
output: Credentials were entered through referenced fields from the latest snapshot.
The browser reached the dashboard URL after submission.
A fresh snapshot confirmed dashboard navigation elements were present.

output_examples.input: Compare the product page before and after expanding the details panel.
output: The baseline screenshot was captured before interaction.
The details panel opened after the referenced control was clicked.
The visual comparison showed changes limited to the expanded details area.

best_practices: Refresh snapshots after navigation, form submission, or major DOM changes.
Use domain allowlists and action policies for sensitive or authenticated workflows.
Store saved browser state securely and rotate it when access changes.
anti_patterns: Reusing old snapshot references after the page changes.
Embedding passwords, tokens, or session files in committed scripts.
Running eval or unrestricted network actions on untrusted pages.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It guides AI agents through browser automation tasks using the agent-browser CLI and deterministic snapshot references.

faq.question: Does it install the browser tool?
faq.answer: No. The agent-browser CLI must already be installed and available to the agent environment.

faq.question: Why should I use snapshot references?
faq.answer: Snapshot references make interactions more repeatable than guessing selectors or clicking by vague page descriptions.

faq.question: Can it handle login workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents environment-based credentials and saved browser state, but those files must be protected.

faq.question: Is JavaScript eval required?
faq.answer: No. Eval is optional and should be disabled with an action policy when working on sensitive or untrusted sites.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because browser automation can navigate, interact, save auth state, and execute page JavaScript.
